{{出典の明記|date=2015年6月}} '''Sandō''' (算道) was the department in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}} under Japan's {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, where the study of {{ill|arithmetic|ja|算術}} was conducted.
== Overview == === Formation Period === Sandō was established at the time of the Daigakuryō's creation under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}. Notable early scholars of Sandō included {{ill|Yamaguchi Tanushi|ja|山口田主}}, {{ill|Shibe Ishimura|ja|私部石村}}, and {{ill|Shibi Mitaji|ja|志斐三田次}}, who were among the educators of the crown prince, later Emperor Shōmu.
Initially, the classification of academic departments within the Daigakuryō was not well-defined.<ref>The designation of specific academic departments within the Daigakuryō, including the use of the term "Sandō," is believed to have begun during the Jōgan period in the Heian era. Before this, the term "Sanka" (算科) was used. However, this article standardizes the terminology to "Sandō."</ref> Unlike the lectures on Confucianism (later known as {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道}}), which served as the main curriculum for training general officials, the disciplines of {{ill|Calligraphy|ja|書道 (大学寮)}} and {{ill|Ondō|ja|音道}} were supplementary. In contrast, Sandō focused on fields unrelated to Confucianism, such as calculation and surveying, to train technical officials, making it the only department independent of the core curriculum. However, this traditional view has been challenged by recent scholarship.
The Daigakuryō had two professors, known as '''Sandokushi''' (算博士, equivalent to Junior Seventh Rank Upper Grade), and 30 students, known as '''Sanshō''' (算生). This was equivalent to the arithmetic studies (算学) at China's {{ill|Guozijian|ja|国子監}}. Compared to the principal department at the Guozijian, which had one professor, two assistants, multiple lecturers, and 400 students, Sandō was smaller. However, the Guozijian itself was significantly larger than the Daigakuryō. Moreover, the Sandokushi in Japan were higher-ranked (Junior Seventh Rank Upper Grade) compared to their Chinese counterparts, who held the lower rank of Junior Ninth Rank Lower Grade.
From the 2nd year of Tenpyō (730), two '''Sandotoku gyōsei''' (算得業生), or special scholarship students, were selected from among the Sanshō as candidates for Sandokushi. Additionally, from the 1st year of Tenpyō-hōji (757), students known as '''Rekisanshō''' (暦算生), who studied arithmetic necessary for calendar studies in the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, were enrolled in Sandō. However, recent scholarship has criticized this understanding, noting the lack of evidence of active Sandokushi in the Nara period, with most references to arithmetic expertise pointing to graduates of {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}}.<ref>For instance, in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Yamaguchi Imiki Tanushi, Shibe Ren Mitaji, and Shibe no Kashira Ishimura, recognized as "masters of arithmetic" in Yōrō 5 (721), were later instructed to teach disciples about planetary movements and calendar distribution in Tenpyō 2 (730). Additionally, Yamaguchi is documented as an "Onmyō Calendar Professor" in two documents from the Wado era.</ref> Some scholars argue that the establishment of Rekisanshō in Tenpyō-hōji 1 was part of a political integration of Sandō into the {{ill|Taishi Bureau|ja|太史局}}.<ref>Hosoi Hiroshi, "The Arithmetic Education System in the Nara Period," *Nihon Rekishi*, Vol. 677, October 2004.</ref>
Within the Daigakuryō, a facility known as the '''Sandōin''' (算道院) was established for Sandō lectures and Sanshō accommodation. After the relocation to {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京}}, Sandōin was located south of the Meikyōdō facilities and north of the facilities for {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}}.<ref>Myōhōdō (initially called Myōhōka) was established alongside the Kidenka (later Kiden-dō) during the Jinki era (724–729).</ref> Available records suggest that many Sanshō came from clans of {{ill|Toraijin|ja|渡来人}} descent.
=== Curriculum === The textbooks for Sandō as defined in the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} included the following nine works: * '''{{ill|Sunzi Suanjing|ja|孫子算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Wucao Suanjing|ja|五曹算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiuzhang Suanshu|ja|九章算術}}''' * '''{{ill|Haidao Suanjing|ja|海島算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Liuzhang|ja|六章}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhuishu|ja|綴術}}''' * '''{{ill|Sankaichōsa|ja|三開重差}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhoubi Suanjing|ja|周髀算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiusi|ja|九司}}'''
While these works partially overlapped with the Chinese Ten Computational Classics (算経十書), certain texts like {{ill|Zhang Qiujian Suanjing|ja|張邱建算経}} were excluded, likely due to transmission circumstances and content relevance.
== Decline of Sandō == Despite its prominence in the early Daigakuryō, Sandō gradually lost status due to shifts in political and educational priorities. By the early Heian period, Sandō had been overshadowed by fields such as {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} and {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}}. The reduction of Sanshō from 30 to 20 in Enryaku 21 (802) marked the beginning of its decline.
== Legacy == Sandō left an indelible mark on Japanese education, but its influence waned as mathematical pursuits fell out of favor until the emergence of {{ill|Wasan|ja|和算}} during the early modern period.
== References == <references />
== See Also == * {{ill|Sanshi|ja|算師}} * {{ill|Mathematics history|ja|数学史}} * {{ill|Wasan|ja|和算}}

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2015年6月}} '''Sandō''' (算道) was the department in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}} under Japan's {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, where the study of {{ill|arithmetic|ja|算術}} was conducted.
== Overview == === Formation Period === Sandō was established at the time of the Daigakuryō's creation under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}. Notable early scholars of Sandō included {{ill|Yamaguchi Tanushi|ja|山口田主}}, {{ill|Shibe Ishimura|ja|私部石村}}, and {{ill|Shibi Mitaji|ja|志斐三田次}}, who were among the educators of the crown prince, later Emperor Shōmu.
Initially, the classification of academic departments within the Daigakuryō was not well-defined.<ref>The designation of specific academic departments within the Daigakuryō, including the use of the term "Sandō," is believed to have begun during the Jōgan period in the Heian era. Before this, the term "Sanka" (算科) was used. However, this article standardizes the terminology to "Sandō."</ref> Unlike the lectures on Confucianism (later known as {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道}}), which served as the main curriculum for training general officials, the disciplines of {{ill|Calligraphy|ja|書道 (大学寮)}} and {{ill|Ondō|ja|音道}} were supplementary. In contrast, Sandō focused on fields unrelated to Confucianism, such as calculation and surveying, to train technical officials, making it the only department independent of the core curriculum. However, this traditional view has been challenged by recent scholarship.
The Daigakuryō had two professors, known as '''Sandokushi''' (算博士, equivalent to Junior Seventh Rank Upper Grade), and 30 students, known as '''Sanshō''' (算生). This was equivalent to the arithmetic studies (算学) at China's {{ill|Guozijian|ja|国子監}}. Compared to the principal department at the Guozijian, which had one professor, two assistants, multiple lecturers, and 400 students, Sandō was smaller. However, the Guozijian itself was significantly larger than the Daigakuryō. Moreover, the Sandokushi in Japan were higher-ranked (Junior Seventh Rank Upper Grade) compared to their Chinese counterparts, who held the lower rank of Junior Ninth Rank Lower Grade.
From the 2nd year of Tenpyō (730), two '''Sandotoku gyōsei''' (算得業生), or special scholarship students, were selected from among the Sanshō as candidates for Sandokushi. Additionally, from the 1st year of Tenpyō-hōji (757), students known as '''Rekisanshō''' (暦算生), who studied arithmetic necessary for calendar studies in the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, were enrolled in Sandō. However, recent scholarship has criticized this understanding, noting the lack of evidence of active Sandokushi in the Nara period, with most references to arithmetic expertise pointing to graduates of {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}}.<ref>For instance, in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Yamaguchi Imiki Tanushi, Shibe Ren Mitaji, and Shibe no Kashira Ishimura, recognized as "masters of arithmetic" in Yōrō 5 (721), were later instructed to teach disciples about planetary movements and calendar distribution in Tenpyō 2 (730). Additionally, Yamaguchi is documented as an "Onmyō Calendar Professor" in two documents from the Wado era.</ref> Some scholars argue that the establishment of Rekisanshō in Tenpyō-hōji 1 was part of a political integration of Sandō into the {{ill|Taishi Bureau|ja|太史局}}.<ref>Hosoi Hiroshi, "The Arithmetic Education System in the Nara Period," *Nihon Rekishi*, Vol. 677, October 2004.</ref>
Within the Daigakuryō, a facility known as the '''Sandōin''' (算道院) was established for Sandō lectures and Sanshō accommodation. After the relocation to {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京}}, Sandōin was located south of the Meikyōdō facilities and north of the facilities for {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}}.<ref>Myōhōdō (initially called Myōhōka) was established alongside the Kidenka (later Kiden-dō) during the Jinki era (724–729).</ref> Available records suggest that many Sanshō came from clans of {{ill|Toraijin|ja|渡来人}} descent.
=== Curriculum === The textbooks for Sandō as defined in the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} included the following nine works: * '''{{ill|Sunzi Suanjing|ja|孫子算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Wucao Suanjing|ja|五曹算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiuzhang Suanshu|ja|九章算術}}''' * '''{{ill|Haidao Suanjing|ja|海島算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Liuzhang|ja|六章}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhuishu|ja|綴術}}''' * '''{{ill|Sankaichōsa|ja|三開重差}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhoubi Suanjing|ja|周髀算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiusi|ja|九司}}'''
While these works partially overlapped with the Chinese Ten Computational Classics (算経十書), certain texts like {{ill|Zhang Qiujian Suanjing|ja|張邱建算経}} were excluded, likely due to transmission circumstances and content relevance.
== Decline of Sandō == Despite its prominence in the early Daigakuryō, Sandō gradually lost status due to shifts in political and educational priorities. By the early Heian period, Sandō had been overshadowed by fields such as {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} and {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}}. The reduction of Sanshō from 30 to 20 in Enryaku 21 (802) marked the beginning of its decline.
== Legacy == Sandō left an indelible mark on Japanese education, but its influence waned as mathematical pursuits fell out of favor until the emergence of {{ill|Wasan|ja|和算}} during the early modern period.
== References == <references />
== See Also == * {{ill|Sanshi|ja|算師}} * {{ill|Mathematics history|ja|数学史}} * {{ill|Wasan|ja|和算}}

{{出典の明記|date=2015年6月}} '''Sandō''' (算道) was the department in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}} under Japan's {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, where the study of {{ill|arithmetic|ja|算術}} was conducted.
== Overview == === Formation Period === Sandō was established at the time of the Daigakuryō's creation under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}. Notable early scholars of Sandō included {{ill|Yamaguchi Tanushi|ja|山口田主}}, {{ill|Shibe Ishimura|ja|私部石村}}, and {{ill|Shibi Mitaji|ja|志斐三田次}}, who were among the educators of the crown prince, later Emperor Shōmu.
Initially, the classification of academic departments within the Daigakuryō was not well-defined.<ref>The designation of specific academic departments within the Daigakuryō, including the use of the term "Sandō," is believed to have begun during the Jōgan period in the Heian era. Before this, the term "Sanka" (算科) was used. However, this article standardizes the terminology to "Sandō."</ref> Unlike the lectures on Confucianism (later known as {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道}}), which served as the main curriculum for training general officials, the disciplines of {{ill|Calligraphy|ja|書道 (大学寮)}} and {{ill|Ondō|ja|音道}} were supplementary. In contrast, Sandō focused on fields unrelated to Confucianism, such as calculation and surveying, to train technical officials, making it the only department independent of the core curriculum. However, this traditional view has been challenged by recent scholarship.
The Daigakuryō had two professors, known as '''Sandokushi''' (算博士, equivalent to Junior Seventh Rank Upper Grade), and 30 students, known as '''Sanshō''' (算生). This was equivalent to the arithmetic studies (算学) at China's {{ill|Guozijian|ja|国子監}}. Compared to the principal department at the Guozijian, which had one professor, two assistants, multiple lecturers, and 400 students, Sandō was smaller. However, the Guozijian itself was significantly larger than the Daigakuryō. Moreover, the Sandokushi in Japan were higher-ranked (Junior Seventh Rank Upper Grade) compared to their Chinese counterparts, who held the lower rank of Junior Ninth Rank Lower Grade.
From the 2nd year of Tenpyō (730), two '''Sandotoku gyōsei''' (算得業生), or special scholarship students, were selected from among the Sanshō as candidates for Sandokushi. Additionally, from the 1st year of Tenpyō-hōji (757), students known as '''Rekisanshō''' (暦算生), who studied arithmetic necessary for calendar studies in the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, were enrolled in Sandō. However, recent scholarship has criticized this understanding, noting the lack of evidence of active Sandokushi in the Nara period, with most references to arithmetic expertise pointing to graduates of {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}}.<ref>For instance, in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Yamaguchi Imiki Tanushi, Shibe Ren Mitaji, and Shibe no Kashira Ishimura, recognized as "masters of arithmetic" in Yōrō 5 (721), were later instructed to teach disciples about planetary movements and calendar distribution in Tenpyō 2 (730). Additionally, Yamaguchi is documented as an "Onmyō Calendar Professor" in two documents from the Wado era.</ref> Some scholars argue that the establishment of Rekisanshō in Tenpyō-hōji 1 was part of a political integration of Sandō into the {{ill|Taishi Bureau|ja|太史局}}.<ref>Hosoi Hiroshi, "The Arithmetic Education System in the Nara Period," *Nihon Rekishi*, Vol. 677, October 2004.</ref>
Within the Daigakuryō, a facility known as the '''Sandōin''' (算道院) was established for Sandō lectures and Sanshō accommodation. After the relocation to {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京}}, Sandōin was located south of the Meikyōdō facilities and north of the facilities for {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}}.<ref>Myōhōdō (initially called Myōhōka) was established alongside the Kidenka (later Kiden-dō) during the Jinki era (724–729).</ref> Available records suggest that many Sanshō came from clans of {{ill|Toraijin|ja|渡来人}} descent.
=== Curriculum === The textbooks for Sandō as defined in the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} included the following nine works: * '''{{ill|Sunzi Suanjing|ja|孫子算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Wucao Suanjing|ja|五曹算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiuzhang Suanshu|ja|九章算術}}''' * '''{{ill|Haidao Suanjing|ja|海島算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Liuzhang|ja|六章}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhuishu|ja|綴術}}''' * '''{{ill|Sankaichōsa|ja|三開重差}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhoubi Suanjing|ja|周髀算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiusi|ja|九司}}'''
While these works partially overlapped with the Chinese Ten Computational Classics (算経十書), certain texts like {{ill|Zhang Qiujian Suanjing|ja|張邱建算経}} were excluded, likely due to transmission circumstances and content relevance.
== Decline of Sandō == Despite its prominence in the early Daigakuryō, Sandō gradually lost status due to shifts in political and educational priorities. By the early Heian period, Sandō had been overshadowed by fields such as {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} and {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}}. The reduction of Sanshō from 30 to 20 in Enryaku 21 (802) marked the beginning of its decline.
== Legacy == Sandō left an indelible mark on Japanese education, but its influence waned as mathematical pursuits fell out of favor until the emergence of {{ill|Wasan|ja|和算}} during the early modern period.
== References == <references />
== See Also == * {{ill|Sanshi|ja|算師}} * {{ill|Mathematics history|ja|数学史}} * {{ill|Wasan|ja|和算}}

In contrast, Sandō focused on fields unrelated to Confucianism, such as calculation and surveying, to train technical officials, making it the only department independent of the core curriculum. However, this traditional view has been challenged by recent scholarship.
The Daigakuryō had two professors, known as '''Sandokushi''' (算博士, equivalent to Junior Seventh Rank Upper Grade), and 30 students, known as '''Sanshō''' (算生). This was equivalent to the arithmetic studies (算学) at China's {{ill|Guozijian|ja|国子監}}. From the 2nd year of Tenpyō (730), two '''Sandotoku gyōsei''' (算得業生), or special scholarship students, were selected from among the Sanshō as candidates for Sandokushi. However, recent scholarship has criticized this understanding, noting the lack of evidence of active Sandokushi in the Nara period, with most references to arithmetic expertise pointing to graduates of {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}}.<ref>For instance, in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Yamaguchi Imiki Tanushi, Shibe Ren Mitaji, and Shibe no Kashira Ishimura, recognized as "masters of arithmetic" in Yōrō 5 (721), were later instructed to teach disciples about planetary movements and calendar distribution in Tenpyō 2 (730). Additionally, Yamaguchi is documented as an "Onmyō Calendar Professor" in two documents from the Wado era.</ref> Some scholars argue that the establishment of Rekisanshō in Tenpyō-hōji 1 was part of a political integration of Sandō into the {{ill|Taishi Bureau|ja|太史局}}.<ref>Hosoi Hiroshi, "The Arithmetic Education System in the Nara Period," *Nihon Rekishi*, Vol. 677, October 2004.</ref>
Within the Daigakuryō, a facility known as the '''Sandōin''' (算道院) was established for Sandō lectures and Sanshō accommodation. After the relocation to {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京}}, Sandōin was located south of the Meikyōdō facilities and north of the facilities for {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}}.<ref>Myōhōdō (initially called Myōhōka) was established alongside the Kidenka (later Kiden-dō) during the Jinki era (724–729).</ref> Available records suggest that many Sanshō came from clans of {{ill|Toraijin|ja|渡来人}} descent.
=== Curriculum === The textbooks for Sandō as defined in the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} included the following nine works: * '''{{ill|Sunzi Suanjing|ja|孫子算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Wucao Suanjing|ja|五曹算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiuzhang Suanshu|ja|九章算術}}''' * '''{{ill|Haidao Suanjing|ja|海島算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Liuzhang|ja|六章}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhuishu|ja|綴術}}''' * '''{{ill|Sankaichōsa|ja|三開重差}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhoubi Suanjing|ja|周髀算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiusi|ja|九司}}'''
While these works partially overlapped with the Chinese Ten Computational Classics (算経十書), certain texts like {{ill|Zhang Qiujian Suanjing|ja|張邱建算経}} were excluded, likely due to transmission circumstances and content relevance.
== Decline of Sandō == Despite its prominence in the early Daigakuryō, Sandō gradually lost status due to shifts in political and educational priorities. By the early Heian period, Sandō had been overshadowed by fields such as {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} and {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}}. The reduction of Sanshō from 30 to 20 in Enryaku 21 (802) marked the beginning of its decline.
== Legacy == Sandō left an indelible mark on Japanese education, but its influence waned as mathematical pursuits fell out of favor until the emergence of {{ill|Wasan|ja|和算}} during the early modern period.

{{出典の明記|date=2015年6月}} '''Sandō''' (算道) was the department in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}} under Japan's {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, where the study of {{ill|arithmetic|ja|算術}} was conducted.
== Overview == === Formation Period === Sandō was established at the time of the Daigakuryō's creation under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}. Notable early scholars of Sandō included {{ill|Yamaguchi Tanushi|ja|山口田主}}, {{ill|Shibe Ishimura|ja|私部石村}}, and {{ill|Shibi Mitaji|ja|志斐三田次}}, who were among the educators of the crown prince, later Emperor Shōmu.
Initially, the classification of academic departments within the Daigakuryō was not well-defined.<ref>The designation of specific academic departments within the Daigakuryō, including the use of the term "Sandō," is believed to have begun during the Jōgan period in the Heian era. Before this, the term "Sanka" (算科) was used. However, this article standardizes the terminology to "Sandō."</ref> Unlike the lectures on Confucianism (later known as {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道}}), which served as the main curriculum for training general officials, the disciplines of {{ill|Calligraphy|ja|書道 (大学寮)}} and {{ill|Ondō|ja|音道}} were supplementary. In contrast, Sandō focused on fields unrelated to Confucianism, such as calculation and surveying, to train technical officials, making it the only department independent of the core curriculum. However, this traditional view has been challenged by recent scholarship.
The Daigakuryō had two professors, known as '''Sandokushi''' (算博士, equivalent to Junior Seventh Rank Upper Grade), and 30 students, known as '''Sanshō''' (算生). This was equivalent to the arithmetic studies (算学) at China's {{ill|Guozijian|ja|国子監}}. Compared to the principal department at the Guozijian, which had one professor, two assistants, multiple lecturers, and 400 students, Sandō was smaller. However, the Guozijian itself was significantly larger than the Daigakuryō. Moreover, the Sandokushi in Japan were higher-ranked (Junior Seventh Rank Upper Grade) compared to their Chinese counterparts, who held the lower rank of Junior Ninth Rank Lower Grade.
From the 2nd year of Tenpyō (730), two '''Sandotoku gyōsei''' (算得業生), or special scholarship students, were selected from among the Sanshō as candidates for Sandokushi. Additionally, from the 1st year of Tenpyō-hōji (757), students known as '''Rekisanshō''' (暦算生), who studied arithmetic necessary for calendar studies in the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, were enrolled in Sandō. However, recent scholarship has criticized this understanding, noting the lack of evidence of active Sandokushi in the Nara period, with most references to arithmetic expertise pointing to graduates of {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}}.<ref>For instance, in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Yamaguchi Imiki Tanushi, Shibe Ren Mitaji, and Shibe no Kashira Ishimura, recognized as "masters of arithmetic" in Yōrō 5 (721), were later instructed to teach disciples about planetary movements and calendar distribution in Tenpyō 2 (730). Additionally, Yamaguchi is documented as an "Onmyō Calendar Professor" in two documents from the Wado era.</ref> Some scholars argue that the establishment of Rekisanshō in Tenpyō-hōji 1 was part of a political integration of Sandō into the {{ill|Taishi Bureau|ja|太史局}}.<ref>Hosoi Hiroshi, "The Arithmetic Education System in the Nara Period," *Nihon Rekishi*, Vol. 677, October 2004.</ref>
Within the Daigakuryō, a facility known as the '''Sandōin''' (算道院) was established for Sandō lectures and Sanshō accommodation. After the relocation to {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京}}, Sandōin was located south of the Meikyōdō facilities and north of the facilities for {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}}.<ref>Myōhōdō (initially called Myōhōka) was established alongside the Kidenka (later Kiden-dō) during the Jinki era (724–729).</ref> Available records suggest that many Sanshō came from clans of {{ill|Toraijin|ja|渡来人}} descent.
=== Curriculum === The textbooks for Sandō as defined in the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} included the following nine works: * '''{{ill|Sunzi Suanjing|ja|孫子算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Wucao Suanjing|ja|五曹算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiuzhang Suanshu|ja|九章算術}}''' * '''{{ill|Haidao Suanjing|ja|海島算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Liuzhang|ja|六章}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhuishu|ja|綴術}}''' * '''{{ill|Sankaichōsa|ja|三開重差}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhoubi Suanjing|ja|周髀算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiusi|ja|九司}}'''
While these works partially overlapped with the Chinese Ten Computational Classics (算経十書), certain texts like {{ill|Zhang Qiujian Suanjing|ja|張邱建算経}} were excluded, likely due to transmission circumstances and content relevance.
== Decline of Sandō == Despite its prominence in the early Daigakuryō, Sandō gradually lost status due to shifts in political and educational priorities. By the early Heian period, Sandō had been overshadowed by fields such as {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} and {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}}. The reduction of Sanshō from 30 to 20 in Enryaku 21 (802) marked the beginning of its decline.
== Legacy == Sandō left an indelible mark on Japanese education, but its influence waned as mathematical pursuits fell out of favor until the emergence of {{ill|Wasan|ja|和算}} during the early modern period.

{{出典の明記|date=2015年6月}} '''Sandō''' (算道) was the department in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}} under Japan's {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, where the study of {{ill|arithmetic|ja|算術}} was conducted.
== Overview == === Formation Period === Sandō was established at the time of the Daigakuryō's creation under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}. Notable early scholars of Sandō included {{ill|Yamaguchi Tanushi|ja|山口田主}}, {{ill|Shibe Ishimura|ja|私部石村}}, and {{ill|Shibi Mitaji|ja|志斐三田次}}, who were among the educators of the crown prince, later Emperor Shōmu.
Initially, the classification of academic departments within the Daigakuryō was not well-defined.<ref>The designation of specific academic departments within the Daigakuryō, including the use of the term "Sandō," is believed to have begun during the Jōgan period in the Heian era. Before this, the term "Sanka" (算科) was used. However, this article standardizes the terminology to "Sandō."</ref> Unlike the lectures on Confucianism (later known as {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道}}), which served as the main curriculum for training general officials, the disciplines of {{ill|Calligraphy|ja|書道 (大学寮)}} and {{ill|Ondō|ja|音道}} were supplementary. In contrast, Sandō focused on fields unrelated to Confucianism, such as calculation and surveying, to train technical officials, making it the only department independent of the core curriculum. However, this traditional view has been challenged by recent scholarship.
The Daigakuryō had two professors, known as '''Sandokushi''' (算博士, equivalent to Junior Seventh Rank Upper Grade), and 30 students, known as '''Sanshō''' (算生). This was equivalent to the arithmetic studies (算学) at China's {{ill|Guozijian|ja|国子監}}. Compared to the principal department at the Guozijian, which had one professor, two assistants, multiple lecturers, and 400 students, Sandō was smaller. However, the Guozijian itself was significantly larger than the Daigakuryō. Moreover, the Sandokushi in Japan were higher-ranked (Junior Seventh Rank Upper Grade) compared to their Chinese counterparts, who held the lower rank of Junior Ninth Rank Lower Grade.
From the 2nd year of Tenpyō (730), two '''Sandotoku gyōsei''' (算得業生), or special scholarship students, were selected from among the Sanshō as candidates for Sandokushi. Additionally, from the 1st year of Tenpyō-hōji (757), students known as '''Rekisanshō''' (暦算生), who studied arithmetic necessary for calendar studies in the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, were enrolled in Sandō. However, recent scholarship has criticized this understanding, noting the lack of evidence of active Sandokushi in the Nara period, with most references to arithmetic expertise pointing to graduates of {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}}.<ref>For instance, in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Yamaguchi Imiki Tanushi, Shibe Ren Mitaji, and Shibe no Kashira Ishimura, recognized as "masters of arithmetic" in Yōrō 5 (721), were later instructed to teach disciples about planetary movements and calendar distribution in Tenpyō 2 (730). Additionally, Yamaguchi is documented as an "Onmyō Calendar Professor" in two documents from the Wado era.</ref> Some scholars argue that the establishment of Rekisanshō in Tenpyō-hōji 1 was part of a political integration of Sandō into the {{ill|Taishi Bureau|ja|太史局}}.<ref>Hosoi Hiroshi, "The Arithmetic Education System in the Nara Period," *Nihon Rekishi*, Vol. 677, October 2004.</ref>
Within the Daigakuryō, a facility known as the '''Sandōin''' (算道院) was established for Sandō lectures and Sanshō accommodation. After the relocation to {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京}}, Sandōin was located south of the Meikyōdō facilities and north of the facilities for {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}}.<ref>Myōhōdō (initially called Myōhōka) was established alongside the Kidenka (later Kiden-dō) during the Jinki era (724–729).</ref> Available records suggest that many Sanshō came from clans of {{ill|Toraijin|ja|渡来人}} descent.
=== Curriculum === The textbooks for Sandō as defined in the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} included the following nine works: * '''{{ill|Sunzi Suanjing|ja|孫子算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Wucao Suanjing|ja|五曹算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiuzhang Suanshu|ja|九章算術}}''' * '''{{ill|Haidao Suanjing|ja|海島算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Liuzhang|ja|六章}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhuishu|ja|綴術}}''' * '''{{ill|Sankaichōsa|ja|三開重差}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhoubi Suanjing|ja|周髀算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiusi|ja|九司}}'''
While these works partially overlapped with the Chinese Ten Computational Classics (算経十書), certain texts like {{ill|Zhang Qiujian Suanjing|ja|張邱建算経}} were excluded, likely due to transmission circumstances and content relevance.
== Decline of Sandō == Despite its prominence in the early Daigakuryō, Sandō gradually lost status due to shifts in political and educational priorities. By the early Heian period, Sandō had been overshadowed by fields such as {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} and {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}}. The reduction of Sanshō from 30 to 20 in Enryaku 21 (802) marked the beginning of its decline.
== Legacy == Sandō left an indelible mark on Japanese education, but its influence waned as mathematical pursuits fell out of favor until the emergence of {{ill|Wasan|ja|和算}} during the early modern period.

Compared to the principal department at the Guozijian, which had one professor, two assistants, multiple lecturers, and 400 students, Sandō was smaller. Moreover, the Sandokushi in Japan were higher-ranked (Junior Seventh Rank Upper Grade) compared to their Chinese counterparts, who held the lower rank of Junior Ninth Rank Lower Grade.
From the 2nd year of Tenpyō (730), two '''Sandotoku gyōsei''' (算得業生), or special scholarship students, were selected from among the Sanshō as candidates for Sandokushi. Additionally, from the 1st year of Tenpyō-hōji (757), students known as '''Rekisanshō''' (暦算生), who studied arithmetic necessary for calendar studies in the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, were enrolled in Sandō. However, recent scholarship has criticized this understanding, noting the lack of evidence of active Sandokushi in the Nara period, with most references to arithmetic expertise pointing to graduates of {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}}.<ref>For instance, in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Yamaguchi Imiki Tanushi, Shibe Ren Mitaji, and Shibe no Kashira Ishimura, recognized as "masters of arithmetic" in Yōrō 5 (721), were later instructed to teach disciples about planetary movements and calendar distribution in Tenpyō 2 (730). Additionally, Yamaguchi is documented as an "Onmyō Calendar Professor" in two documents from the Wado era.</ref> Some scholars argue that the establishment of Rekisanshō in Tenpyō-hōji 1 was part of a political integration of Sandō into the {{ill|Taishi Bureau|ja|太史局}}.<ref>Hosoi Hiroshi, "The Arithmetic Education System in the Nara Period," *Nihon Rekishi*, Vol. 677, October 2004.</ref>
Within the Daigakuryō, a facility known as the '''Sandōin''' (算道院) was established for Sandō lectures and Sanshō accommodation. After the relocation to {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京}}, Sandōin was located south of the Meikyōdō facilities and north of the facilities for {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}}.<ref>Myōhōdō (initially called Myōhōka) was established alongside the Kidenka (later Kiden-dō) during the Jinki era (724–729).</ref> Available records suggest that many Sanshō came from clans of {{ill|Toraijin|ja|渡来人}} descent.
=== Curriculum === The textbooks for Sandō as defined in the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} included the following nine works: * '''{{ill|Sunzi Suanjing|ja|孫子算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Wucao Suanjing|ja|五曹算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiuzhang Suanshu|ja|九章算術}}''' * '''{{ill|Haidao Suanjing|ja|海島算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Liuzhang|ja|六章}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhuishu|ja|綴術}}''' * '''{{ill|Sankaichōsa|ja|三開重差}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhoubi Suanjing|ja|周髀算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiusi|ja|九司}}'''
While these works partially overlapped with the Chinese Ten Computational Classics (算経十書), certain texts like {{ill|Zhang Qiujian Suanjing|ja|張邱建算経}} were excluded, likely due to transmission circumstances and content relevance.
== Decline of Sandō == Despite its prominence in the early Daigakuryō, Sandō gradually lost status due to shifts in political and educational priorities. By the early Heian period, Sandō had been overshadowed by fields such as {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} and {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}}. The reduction of Sanshō from 30 to 20 in Enryaku 21 (802) marked the beginning of its decline.
== Legacy == Sandō left an indelible mark on Japanese education, but its influence waned as mathematical pursuits fell out of favor until the emergence of {{ill|Wasan|ja|和算}} during the early modern period.

{{出典の明記|date=2015年6月}} '''Sandō''' (算道) was the department in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}} under Japan's {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, where the study of {{ill|arithmetic|ja|算術}} was conducted.
== Overview == === Formation Period === Sandō was established at the time of the Daigakuryō's creation under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}. Notable early scholars of Sandō included {{ill|Yamaguchi Tanushi|ja|山口田主}}, {{ill|Shibe Ishimura|ja|私部石村}}, and {{ill|Shibi Mitaji|ja|志斐三田次}}, who were among the educators of the crown prince, later Emperor Shōmu.
Initially, the classification of academic departments within the Daigakuryō was not well-defined.<ref>The designation of specific academic departments within the Daigakuryō, including the use of the term "Sandō," is believed to have begun during the Jōgan period in the Heian era. Before this, the term "Sanka" (算科) was used. However, this article standardizes the terminology to "Sandō."</ref> Unlike the lectures on Confucianism (later known as {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道}}), which served as the main curriculum for training general officials, the disciplines of {{ill|Calligraphy|ja|書道 (大学寮)}} and {{ill|Ondō|ja|音道}} were supplementary. In contrast, Sandō focused on fields unrelated to Confucianism, such as calculation and surveying, to train technical officials, making it the only department independent of the core curriculum. However, this traditional view has been challenged by recent scholarship.
The Daigakuryō had two professors, known as '''Sandokushi''' (算博士, equivalent to Junior Seventh Rank Upper Grade), and 30 students, known as '''Sanshō''' (算生). This was equivalent to the arithmetic studies (算学) at China's {{ill|Guozijian|ja|国子監}}. Compared to the principal department at the Guozijian, which had one professor, two assistants, multiple lecturers, and 400 students, Sandō was smaller. However, the Guozijian itself was significantly larger than the Daigakuryō. Moreover, the Sandokushi in Japan were higher-ranked (Junior Seventh Rank Upper Grade) compared to their Chinese counterparts, who held the lower rank of Junior Ninth Rank Lower Grade.
From the 2nd year of Tenpyō (730), two '''Sandotoku gyōsei''' (算得業生), or special scholarship students, were selected from among the Sanshō as candidates for Sandokushi. Additionally, from the 1st year of Tenpyō-hōji (757), students known as '''Rekisanshō''' (暦算生), who studied arithmetic necessary for calendar studies in the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, were enrolled in Sandō. However, recent scholarship has criticized this understanding, noting the lack of evidence of active Sandokushi in the Nara period, with most references to arithmetic expertise pointing to graduates of {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道}}.<ref>For instance, in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Yamaguchi Imiki Tanushi, Shibe Ren Mitaji, and Shibe no Kashira Ishimura, recognized as "masters of arithmetic" in Yōrō 5 (721), were later instructed to teach disciples about planetary movements and calendar distribution in Tenpyō 2 (730). Additionally, Yamaguchi is documented as an "Onmyō Calendar Professor" in two documents from the Wado era.</ref> Some scholars argue that the establishment of Rekisanshō in Tenpyō-hōji 1 was part of a political integration of Sandō into the {{ill|Taishi Bureau|ja|太史局}}.<ref>Hosoi Hiroshi, "The Arithmetic Education System in the Nara Period," *Nihon Rekishi*, Vol. 677, October 2004.</ref>
Within the Daigakuryō, a facility known as the '''Sandōin''' (算道院) was established for Sandō lectures and Sanshō accommodation. After the relocation to {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京}}, Sandōin was located south of the Meikyōdō facilities and north of the facilities for {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}}.<ref>Myōhōdō (initially called Myōhōka) was established alongside the Kidenka (later Kiden-dō) during the Jinki era (724–729).</ref> Available records suggest that many Sanshō came from clans of {{ill|Toraijin|ja|渡来人}} descent.
=== Curriculum === The textbooks for Sandō as defined in the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} included the following nine works: * '''{{ill|Sunzi Suanjing|ja|孫子算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Wucao Suanjing|ja|五曹算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiuzhang Suanshu|ja|九章算術}}''' * '''{{ill|Haidao Suanjing|ja|海島算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Liuzhang|ja|六章}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhuishu|ja|綴術}}''' * '''{{ill|Sankaichōsa|ja|三開重差}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhoubi Suanjing|ja|周髀算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiusi|ja|九司}}'''
While these works partially overlapped with the Chinese Ten Computational Classics (算経十書), certain texts like {{ill|Zhang Qiujian Suanjing|ja|張邱建算経}} were excluded, likely due to transmission circumstances and content relevance.
== Decline of Sandō == Despite its prominence in the early Daigakuryō, Sandō gradually lost status due to shifts in political and educational priorities. By the early Heian period, Sandō had been overshadowed by fields such as {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} and {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}}. The reduction of Sanshō from 30 to 20 in Enryaku 21 (802) marked the beginning of its decline.
== Legacy == Sandō left an indelible mark on Japanese education, but its influence waned as mathematical pursuits fell out of favor until the emergence of {{ill|Wasan|ja|和算}} during the early modern period.
== References == <references />
== See Also == * {{ill|Sanshi|ja|算師}} * {{ill|Mathematics history|ja|数学史}} * {{ill|Wasan|ja|和算}}

{{出典の明記|date=2015年6月}} '''Sandō''' (算道) was the department in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}} under Japan's {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, where the study of {{ill|arithmetic|ja|算術}} was conducted.
== Overview == === Formation Period === Sandō was established at the time of the Daigakuryō's creation under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}. Notable early scholars of Sandō included {{ill|Yamaguchi Tanushi|ja|山口田主}}, {{ill|Shibe Ishimura|ja|私部石村}}, and {{ill|Shibi Mitaji|ja|志斐三田次}}, who were among the educators of the crown prince, later Emperor Shōmu.
Initially, the classification of academic departments within the Daigakuryō was not well-defined.<ref>The designation of specific academic departments within the Daigakuryō, including the use of the term "Sandō," is believed to have begun during the Jōgan period in the Heian era. Before this, the term "Sanka" (算科) was used. However, this article standardizes the terminology to "Sandō."</ref> Unlike the lectures on Confucianism (later known as {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道}}), which served as the main curriculum for training general officials, the disciplines of {{ill|Calligraphy|ja|書道 (大学寮)}} and {{ill|Ondō|ja|音道}} were supplementary. In contrast, Sandō focused on fields unrelated to Confucianism, such as calculation and surveying, to train technical officials, making it the only department independent of the core curriculum. However, this traditional view has been challenged by recent scholarship.
The Daigakuryō had two professors, known as '''Sandokushi''' (算博士, equivalent to Junior Seventh Rank Upper Grade), and 30 students, known as '''Sanshō''' (算生). This was equivalent to the arithmetic studies (算学) at China's {{ill|Guozijian|ja|国子監}}. Compared to the principal department at the Guozijian, which had one professor, two assistants, multiple lecturers, and 400 students, Sandō was smaller. However, the Guozijian itself was significantly larger than the Daigakuryō. Moreover, the Sandokushi in Japan were higher-ranked (Junior Seventh Rank Upper Grade) compared to their Chinese counterparts, who held the lower rank of Junior Ninth Rank Lower Grade.
From the 2nd year of Tenpyō (730), two '''Sandotoku gyōsei''' (算得業生), or special scholarship students, were selected from among the Sanshō as candidates for Sandokushi. However, recent scholarship has criticized this understanding, noting the lack of evidence of active Sandokushi in the Nara period, with most references to arithmetic expertise pointing to graduates of {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō}}.<ref>For instance, in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Yamaguchi Imiki Tanushi, Shibe Ren Mitaji, and Shibe no Kashira Ishimura, recognized as "masters of arithmetic" in Yōrō 5 (721), were later instructed to teach disciples about planetary movements and calendar distribution in Tenpyō 2 (730). Additionally, Yamaguchi is documented as an "Onmyō Calendar Professor" in two documents from the Wado era.</ref> Some scholars argue that the establishment of Rekisanshō in Tenpyō-hōji 1 was part of a political integration of Sandō into the {{ill|Taishi Bureau|ja|太史局}}.<ref>Hosoi Hiroshi, "The Arithmetic Education System in the Nara Period," *Nihon Rekishi*, Vol. 677, October 2004.</ref>
Within the Daigakuryō, a facility known as the '''Sandōin''' (算道院) was established for Sandō lectures and Sanshō accommodation. After the relocation to {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京}}, Sandōin was located south of the Meikyōdō facilities and north of the facilities for {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}}.<ref>Myōhōdō (initially called Myōhōka) was established alongside the Kidenka (later Kiden-dō) during the Jinki era (724–729).</ref> Available records suggest that many Sanshō came from clans of {{ill|Toraijin|ja|渡来人}} descent.
=== Curriculum === The textbooks for Sandō as defined in the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} included the following nine works: * '''{{ill|Sunzi Suanjing|ja|孫子算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Wucao Suanjing|ja|五曹算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiuzhang Suanshu|ja|九章算術}}''' * '''{{ill|Haidao Suanjing|ja|海島算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Liuzhang|ja|六章}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhuishu|ja|綴術}}''' * '''{{ill|Sankaichōsa|ja|三開重差}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhoubi Suanjing|ja|周髀算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiusi|ja|九司}}'''
While these works partially overlapped with the Chinese Ten Computational Classics (算経十書), certain texts like {{ill|Zhang Qiujian Suanjing|ja|張邱建算経}} were excluded, likely due to transmission circumstances and content relevance.
== Decline of Sandō == Despite its prominence in the early Daigakuryō, Sandō gradually lost status due to shifts in political and educational priorities. By the early Heian period, Sandō had been overshadowed by fields such as {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} and {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}}. The reduction of Sanshō from 30 to 20 in Enryaku 21 (802) marked the beginning of its decline.
== Legacy == Sandō left an indelible mark on Japanese education, but its influence waned as mathematical pursuits fell out of favor until the emergence of {{ill|Wasan|ja|和算}} during the early modern period.

While these works partially overlapped with the Chinese Ten Computational Classics (算経十書), certain texts like {{ill|Zhang Qiujian Suanjing|ja|張邱建算経}} were excluded, likely due to transmission circumstances and content relevance.
== Decline of Sandō == Despite its prominence in the early Daigakuryō, Sandō gradually lost status due to shifts in political and educational priorities. By the early Heian period, Sandō had been overshadowed by fields such as {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} and {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}}. The reduction of Sanshō from 30 to 20 in Enryaku 21 (802) marked the beginning of its decline.
== Legacy == Sandō left an indelible mark on Japanese education, but its influence waned as mathematical pursuits fell out of favor until the emergence of {{ill|Wasan|ja|和算}} during the early modern period.
== References == <references />
== See Also == * {{ill|Sanshi|ja|算師}} * {{ill|Mathematics history|ja|数学史}} * {{ill|Wasan|ja|和算}}

{{出典の明記|date=2015年6月}} '''Sandō''' (算道) was the department in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}} under Japan's {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, where the study of {{ill|arithmetic|ja|算術}} was conducted.
== Overview == === Formation Period === Sandō was established at the time of the Daigakuryō's creation under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}. Notable early scholars of Sandō included {{ill|Yamaguchi Tanushi|ja|山口田主}}, {{ill|Shibe Ishimura|ja|私部石村}}, and {{ill|Shibi Mitaji|ja|志斐三田次}}, who were among the educators of the crown prince, later Emperor Shōmu.
Initially, the classification of academic departments within the Daigakuryō was not well-defined.<ref>The designation of specific academic departments within the Daigakuryō, including the use of the term "Sandō," is believed to have begun during the Jōgan period in the Heian era. Before this, the term "Sanka" (算科) was used. However, this article standardizes the terminology to "Sandō."</ref> Unlike the lectures on Confucianism (later known as {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道}}), which served as the main curriculum for training general officials, the disciplines of {{ill|Calligraphy|ja|書道 (大学寮)}} and {{ill|Ondō|ja|音道}} were supplementary. In contrast, Sandō focused on fields unrelated to Confucianism, such as calculation and surveying, to train technical officials, making it the only department independent of the core curriculum. However, this traditional view has been challenged by recent scholarship.
The Daigakuryō had two professors, known as '''Sandokushi''' (算博士, equivalent to Junior Seventh Rank Upper Grade), and 30 students, known as '''Sanshō''' (算生). This was equivalent to the arithmetic studies (算学) at China's {{ill|Guozijian|ja|国子監}}. Compared to the principal department at the Guozijian, which had one professor, two assistants, multiple lecturers, and 400 students, Sandō was smaller. However, the Guozijian itself was significantly larger than the Daigakuryō. Moreover, the Sandokushi in Japan were higher-ranked (Junior Seventh Rank Upper Grade) compared to their Chinese counterparts, who held the lower rank of Junior Ninth Rank Lower Grade.
From the 2nd year of Tenpyō (730), two '''Sandotoku gyōsei''' (算得業生), or special scholarship students, were selected from among the Sanshō as candidates for Sandokushi. Additionally, from the 1st year of Tenpyō-hōji (757), students known as '''Rekisanshō''' (暦算生), who studied arithmetic necessary for calendar studies in the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, were enrolled in Sandō. However, recent scholarship has criticized this understanding, noting the lack of evidence of active Sandokushi in the Nara period, with most references to arithmetic expertise pointing to graduates of {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō}}.<ref>For instance, in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Yamaguchi Imiki Tanushi, Shibe Ren Mitaji, and Shibe no Kashira Ishimura, recognized as "masters of arithmetic" in Yōrō 5 (721), were later instructed to teach disciples about planetary movements and calendar distribution in Tenpyō 2 (730). Additionally, Yamaguchi is documented as an "Onmyō Calendar Professor" in two documents from the Wado era.</ref> Some scholars argue that the establishment of Rekisanshō in Tenpyō-hōji 1 was part of a political integration of Sandō into the {{ill|Taishi Bureau|ja|太史局}}.<ref>Hosoi Hiroshi, "The Arithmetic Education System in the Nara Period," *Nihon Rekishi*, Vol. 677, October 2004.</ref>
Within the Daigakuryō, a facility known as the '''Sandōin''' (算道院) was established for Sandō lectures and Sanshō accommodation. After the relocation to {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京}}, Sandōin was located south of the Meikyōdō facilities and north of the facilities for {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}}.<ref>Myōhōdō (initially called Myōhōka) was established alongside the Kidenka (later Kiden-dō) during the Jinki era (724–729).</ref> Available records suggest that many Sanshō came from clans of {{ill|Toraijin|ja|渡来人}} descent.
=== Curriculum === The textbooks for Sandō as defined in the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} included the following nine works: * '''{{ill|Sunzi Suanjing|ja|孫子算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Wucao Suanjing|ja|五曹算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiuzhang Suanshu|ja|九章算術}}''' * '''{{ill|Haidao Suanjing|ja|海島算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Liuzhang|ja|六章}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhuishu|ja|綴術}}''' * '''{{ill|Sankaichōsa|ja|三開重差}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhoubi Suanjing|ja|周髀算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiusi|ja|九司}}'''
While these works partially overlapped with the Chinese Ten Computational Classics (算経十書), certain texts like {{ill|Zhang Qiujian Suanjing|ja|張邱建算経}} were excluded, likely due to transmission circumstances and content relevance.
== Decline of Sandō == Despite its prominence in the early Daigakuryō, Sandō gradually lost status due to shifts in political and educational priorities. By the early Heian period, Sandō had been overshadowed by fields such as {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} and {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}}. The reduction of Sanshō from 30 to 20 in Enryaku 21 (802) marked the beginning of its decline.
== Legacy == Sandō left an indelible mark on Japanese education, but its influence waned as mathematical pursuits fell out of favor until the emergence of {{ill|Wasan|ja|和算}} during the early modern period.
== References == <references />
== See Also == * {{ill|Sanshi|ja|算師}} * {{ill|Mathematics history|ja|数学史}} * {{ill|Wasan|ja|和算}}

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2015年6月}} '''Sandō''' (算道) was the department in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}} under Japan's {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, where the study of {{ill|arithmetic|ja|算術}} was conducted.
== Overview == === Formation Period === Sandō was established at the time of the Daigakuryō's creation under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}. Notable early scholars of Sandō included {{ill|Yamaguchi Tanushi|ja|山口田主}}, {{ill|Shibe Ishimura|ja|私部石村}}, and {{ill|Shibi Mitaji|ja|志斐三田次}}, who were among the educators of the crown prince, later Emperor Shōmu.
Initially, the classification of academic departments within the Daigakuryō was not well-defined.<ref>The designation of specific academic departments within the Daigakuryō, including the use of the term "Sandō," is believed to have begun during the Jōgan period in the Heian era. Before this, the term "Sanka" (算科) was used. However, this article standardizes the terminology to "Sandō."</ref> Unlike the lectures on Confucianism (later known as {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道}}), which served as the main curriculum for training general officials, the disciplines of {{ill|Calligraphy|ja|書道 (大学寮)}} and {{ill|Ondō|ja|音道}} were supplementary. In contrast, Sandō focused on fields unrelated to Confucianism, such as calculation and surveying, to train technical officials, making it the only department independent of the core curriculum. However, this traditional view has been challenged by recent scholarship.
The Daigakuryō had two professors, known as '''Sandokushi''' (算博士, equivalent to Junior Seventh Rank Upper Grade), and 30 students, known as '''Sanshō''' (算生). This was equivalent to the arithmetic studies (算学) at China's {{ill|Guozijian|ja|国子監}}. Compared to the principal department at the Guozijian, which had one professor, two assistants, multiple lecturers, and 400 students, Sandō was smaller. However, the Guozijian itself was significantly larger than the Daigakuryō. Moreover, the Sandokushi in Japan were higher-ranked (Junior Seventh Rank Upper Grade) compared to their Chinese counterparts, who held the lower rank of Junior Ninth Rank Lower Grade.
From the 2nd year of Tenpyō (730), two '''Sandotoku gyōsei''' (算得業生), or special scholarship students, were selected from among the Sanshō as candidates for Sandokushi. Additionally, from the 1st year of Tenpyō-hōji (757), students known as '''Rekisanshō''' (暦算生), who studied arithmetic necessary for calendar studies in the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, were enrolled in Sandō. However, recent scholarship has criticized this understanding, noting the lack of evidence of active Sandokushi in the Nara period, with most references to arithmetic expertise pointing to graduates of {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō}}.<ref>For instance, in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Yamaguchi Imiki Tanushi, Shibe Ren Mitaji, and Shibe no Kashira Ishimura, recognized as "masters of arithmetic" in Yōrō 5 (721), were later instructed to teach disciples about planetary movements and calendar distribution in Tenpyō 2 (730). Additionally, Yamaguchi is documented as an "Onmyō Calendar Professor" in two documents from the Wado era.</ref> Some scholars argue that the establishment of Rekisanshō in Tenpyō-hōji 1 was part of a political integration of Sandō into the {{ill|Taishi Bureau|ja|太史局}}.<ref>Hosoi Hiroshi, "The Arithmetic Education System in the Nara Period," *Nihon Rekishi*, Vol. 677, October 2004.</ref>
Within the Daigakuryō, a facility known as the '''Sandōin''' (算道院) was established for Sandō lectures and Sanshō accommodation. After the relocation to {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京}}, Sandōin was located south of the Meikyōdō facilities and north of the facilities for {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}}.<ref>Myōhōdō (initially called Myōhōka) was established alongside the Kidenka (later Kiden-dō) during the Jinki era (724–729).</ref> Available records suggest that many Sanshō came from clans of {{ill|Toraijin|ja|渡来人}} descent.
=== Curriculum === The textbooks for Sandō as defined in the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} included the following nine works: * '''{{ill|Sunzi Suanjing|ja|孫子算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Wucao Suanjing|ja|五曹算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiuzhang Suanshu|ja|九章算術}}''' * '''{{ill|Haidao Suanjing|ja|海島算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Liuzhang|ja|六章}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhuishu|ja|綴術}}''' * '''{{ill|Sankaichōsa|ja|三開重差}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhoubi Suanjing|ja|周髀算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiusi|ja|九司}}'''
While these works partially overlapped with the Chinese Ten Computational Classics (算経十書), certain texts like {{ill|Zhang Qiujian Suanjing|ja|張邱建算経}} were excluded, likely due to transmission circumstances and content relevance.
== Decline of Sandō == Despite its prominence in the early Daigakuryō, Sandō gradually lost status due to shifts in political and educational priorities. By the early Heian period, Sandō had been overshadowed by fields such as {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} and {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}}. The reduction of Sanshō from 30 to 20 in Enryaku 21 (802) marked the beginning of its decline.
== Legacy == Sandō left an indelible mark on Japanese education, but its influence waned as mathematical pursuits fell out of favor until the emergence of {{ill|Wasan|ja|和算}} during the early modern period.
== References == <references /> == See Also! == * {{ill|Sanshi|ja|算師}} * {{ill|Mathematics history|ja|数学史}} * {{ill|Wasan|ja|和算}}

Initially, the classification of academic departments within the Daigakuryō was not well-defined.<ref>The designation of specific academic departments within the Daigakuryō, including the use of the term "Sandō," is believed to have begun during the Jōgan period in the Heian era. Before this, the term "Sanka" (算科) was used. However, this article standardizes the terminology to "Sandō."</ref> Unlike the lectures on Confucianism (later known as {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道}}), which served as the main curriculum for training general officials, the disciplines of {{ill|Calligraphy|ja|書道 (大学寮)}} and {{ill|Ondō|ja|音道}} were supplementary. In contrast, Sandō focused on fields unrelated to Confucianism, such as calculation and surveying, to train technical officials, making it the only department independent of the core curriculum. However, this traditional view has been challenged by recent scholarship.
The Daigakuryō had two professors, known as '''Sandokushi''' (算博士, equivalent to Junior Seventh Rank Upper Grade), and 30 students, known as '''Sanshō''' (算生). This was equivalent to the arithmetic studies (算学) at China's {{ill|Guozijian|ja|国子監}}. Compared to the principal department at the Guozijian, which had one professor, two assistants, multiple lecturers, and 400 students, Sandō was smaller. However, the Guozijian itself was significantly larger than the Daigakuryō. Moreover, the Sandokushi in Japan were higher-ranked (Junior Seventh Rank Upper Grade) compared to their Chinese counterparts, who held the lower rank of Junior Ninth Rank Lower Grade.
From the 2nd year of Tenpyō (730), two '''Sandotoku gyōsei''' (算得業生), or special scholarship students, were selected from among the Sanshō as candidates for Sandokushi. Additionally, from the 1st year of Tenpyō-hōji (757), students known as '''Rekisanshō''' (暦算生), who studied arithmetic necessary for calendar studies in the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, were enrolled in Sandō. However, recent scholarship has criticized this understanding, noting the lack of evidence of active Sandokushi in the Nara period, with most references to arithmetic expertise pointing to graduates of {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō}}.<ref>For instance, in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Yamaguchi Imiki Tanushi, Shibe Ren Mitaji, and Shibe no Kashira Ishimura, recognized as "masters of arithmetic" in Yōrō 5 (721), were later instructed to teach disciples about planetary movements and calendar distribution in Tenpyō 2 (730). Additionally, Yamaguchi is documented as an "Onmyō Calendar Professor" in two documents from the Wado era.</ref> Some scholars argue that the establishment of Rekisanshō in Tenpyō-hōji 1 was part of a political integration of Sandō into the {{ill|Taishi Bureau|ja|太史局}}.<ref>Hosoi Hiroshi, "The Arithmetic Education System in the Nara Period," *Nihon Rekishi*, Vol. 677, October 2004.</ref>
Within the Daigakuryō, a facility known as the '''Sandōin''' (算道院) was established for Sandō lectures and Sanshō accommodation. After the relocation to {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京}}, Sandōin was located south of the Meikyōdō facilities and north of the facilities for {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}}.<ref>Myōhōdō (initially called Myōhōka) was established alongside the Kidenka (later Kiden-dō) during the Jinki era (724–729).</ref> Available records suggest that many Sanshō came from clans of {{ill|Toraijin|ja|渡来人}} descent.
=== Curriculum === The textbooks for Sandō as defined in the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} included the following nine works: * '''{{ill|Sunzi Suanjing|ja|孫子算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Wucao Suanjing|ja|五曹算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiuzhang Suanshu|ja|九章算術}}''' * '''{{ill|Haidao Suanjing|ja|海島算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Liuzhang|ja|六章}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhuishu|ja|綴術}}''' * '''{{ill|Sankaichōsa|ja|三開重差}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhoubi Suanjing|ja|周髀算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiusi|ja|九司}}'''
While these works partially overlapped with the Chinese Ten Computational Classics (算経十書), certain texts like {{ill|Zhang Qiujian Suanjing|ja|張邱建算経}} were excluded, likely due to transmission circumstances and content relevance.
== Decline of Sandō == Despite its prominence in the early Daigakuryō, Sandō gradually lost status due to shifts in political and educational priorities. By the early Heian period, Sandō had been overshadowed by fields such as {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} and {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}}. The reduction of Sanshō from 30 to 20 in Enryaku 21 (802) marked the beginning of its decline.
== Legacy == Sandō left an indelible mark on Japanese education, but its influence waned as mathematical pursuits fell out of favor until the emergence of {{ill|Wasan|ja|和算}} during the early modern period.
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Sanshi|ja|算師}} * {{ill|Mathematics history|ja|数学史}} * {{ill|Wasan|ja|和算}}

{{出典の明記|date=2015th year 6月}} '''Sandō''' (算道) was the department in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}} under Japan's {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, where the study of {{ill|arithmetic|ja|算術}} was conducted.
== Overview == === Formation Period === Sandō was established at the time of the Daigakuryō's creation under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}. Notable early scholars of Sandō included {{ill|Yamaguchi Tanushi|ja|山口田主}}, {{ill|Shibe Ishimura|ja|私部石村}}, and {{ill|Shibi Mitaji|ja|志斐三田次}}, who were among the educators of the crown prince, later Emperor Shōmu.
Initially, the classification of academic departments within the Daigakuryō was not well-defined.<ref>The designation of specific academic departments within the Daigakuryō, including the use of the term "Sandō," is believed to have begun during the Jōgan period in the Heian era. Before this, the term "Sanka" (算科) was used. However, this article standardizes the terminology to "Sandō."</ref> Unlike the lectures on Confucianism (later known as {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道}}), which served as the main curriculum for training general officials, the disciplines of {{ill|Calligraphy|ja|書道 (大学寮)}} and {{ill|Ondō|ja|音道}} were supplementary. In contrast, Sandō focused on fields unrelated to Confucianism, such as calculation and surveying, to train technical officials, making it the only department independent of the core curriculum. However, this traditional view has been challenged by recent scholarship.
The Daigakuryō had two professors, known as '''Sandokushi''' (算博士, equivalent to Junior Seventh Rank Upper Grade), and 30 students, known as '''Sanshō''' (算生). This was equivalent to the arithmetic studies (算学) at China's {{ill|Guozijian|ja|国子監}}. Compared to the principal department at the Guozijian, which had one professor, two assistants, multiple lecturers, and 400 students, Sandō was smaller. However, the Guozijian itself was significantly larger than the Daigakuryō. Moreover, the Sandokushi in Japan were higher-ranked (Junior Seventh Rank Upper Grade) compared to their Chinese counterparts, who held the lower rank of Junior Ninth Rank Lower Grade.
From the 2nd year of Tenpyō (730), two '''Sandotoku gyōsei''' (算得業生), or special scholarship students, were selected from among the Sanshō as candidates for Sandokushi. Additionally, from the 1st year of Tenpyō-hōji (757), students known as '''Rekisanshō''' (暦算生), who studied arithmetic necessary for calendar studies in the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}, were enrolled in Sandō. However, recent scholarship has criticized this understanding, noting the lack of evidence of active Sandokushi in the Nara period, with most references to arithmetic expertise pointing to graduates of {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō}}.<ref>For instance, in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Yamaguchi Imiki Tanushi, Shibe Ren Mitaji, and Shibe no Kashira Ishimura, recognized as "masters of arithmetic" in Yōrō 5 (721), were later instructed to teach disciples about planetary movements and calendar distribution in Tenpyō 2 (730). Additionally, Yamaguchi is documented as an "Onmyō Calendar Professor" in two documents from the Wado era.</ref> Some scholars argue that the establishment of Rekisanshō in Tenpyō-hōji 1 was part of a political integration of Sandō into the {{ill|Taishi Bureau|ja|太史局}}.<ref>Hosoi Hiroshi, "The Arithmetic Education System in the Nara Period," *Nihon Rekishi*, Vol. 677, October 2004.</ref>
Within the Daigakuryō, a facility known as the '''Sandōin''' (算道院) was established for Sandō lectures and Sanshō accommodation. After the relocation to {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京}}, Sandōin was located south of the Meikyōdō facilities and north of the facilities for {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}}.<ref>Myōhōdō (initially called Myōhōka) was established alongside the Kidenka (later Kiden-dō) during the Jinki era (724–729).</ref> Available records suggest that many Sanshō came from clans of {{ill|Toraijin|ja|渡来人}} descent.
=== Curriculum === The textbooks for Sandō as defined in the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} included the following nine works: * '''{{ill|Sunzi Suanjing|ja|孫子算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Wucao Suanjing|ja|五曹算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiuzhang Suanshu|ja|九章算術}}''' * '''{{ill|Haidao Suanjing|ja|海島算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Liuzhang|ja|六章}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhuishu|ja|綴術}}''' * '''{{ill|Sankaichōsa|ja|三開重差}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhoubi Suanjing|ja|周髀算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiusi|ja|九司}}'''
While these works partially overlapped with the Chinese Ten Computational Classics (算経十書), certain texts like {{ill|Zhang Qiujian Suanjing|ja|張邱建算経}} were excluded, likely due to transmission circumstances and content relevance.

By the early Heian period, Sandō had been overshadowed by fields such as {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} and {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}}. The reduction of Sanshō from 30 to 20 in Enryaku 21 (802) marked the beginning of its decline.

{{出典の明記|date=2015th year 6月}} '''Sandō''' (算道) was the department in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮}} under Japan's {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, where the study of {{ill|arithmetic|ja|算術}} was conducted.

Additionally, Yamaguchi is documented as an "Onmyō Calendar Professor" in two documents from the Wado era.</ref> Some scholars argue that the establishment of Rekisanshō in Tenpyō-hōji 1 was part of a political integration of Sandō into the {{ill|Taishi Bureau|ja|太史局}}.<ref>Hosoi Hiroshi, "The Arithmetic Education System in the Nara Period," *Nihon Rekishi*, Vol. 677, October 2004.</ref>
Within the Daigakuryō, a facility known as the '''Sandōin''' (算道院) was established for Sandō lectures and Sanshō accommodation. After the relocation to {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京}}, Sandōin was located south of the Meikyōdō facilities and north of the facilities for {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}}.<ref>Myōhōdō (initially called Myōhōka) was established alongside the Kidenka (later Kiden-dō) during the Jinki era (724–729).</ref> Available records suggest that many Sanshō came from clans of {{ill|Toraijin|ja|渡来人}} descent.
=== Curriculum === The textbooks for Sandō as defined in the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} included the following nine works: * '''{{ill|Sunzi Suanjing|ja|孫子算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Wucao Suanjing|ja|五曹算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiuzhang Suanshu|ja|九章算術}}''' * '''{{ill|Haidao Suanjing|ja|海島算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Liuzhang|ja|六章}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhuishu|ja|綴術}}''' * '''{{ill|Sankaichōsa|ja|三開重差}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhoubi Suanjing|ja|周髀算経}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiusi|ja|九司}}'''
While these works partially overlapped with the Chinese Ten Computational Classics (算経十書), certain texts like {{ill|Zhang Qiujian Suanjing|ja|張邱建算経}} were excluded, likely due to transmission circumstances and content relevance.
== Decline of Sandō == Despite its prominence in the early Daigakuryō, Sandō gradually lost status due to shifts in political and educational priorities. By the early Heian period, Sandō had been overshadowed by fields such as {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道}} and {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道}}. The reduction of Sanshō from 30 to 20 in Enryaku 21 (802) marked the beginning of its decline.
== Legacy == Sandō left an indelible mark on Japanese education, but its influence waned as mathematical pursuits fell out of favor until the emergence of {{ill|Wasan|ja|和算}} during the early modern period.

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2015th year 6月}} '''Sandō''' (算道) was the department in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮|12=draft}} under Japan's {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|12=draft}}, where the study of {{ill|arithmetic|ja|算術|12=draft}} was conducted.
== Overview == === Formation Period === Sandō was established at the time of the Daigakuryō's creation under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令|12=draft}}. Notable early scholars of Sandō included {{ill|Yamaguchi Tanushi|ja|山口田主|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shibe Ishimura|ja|私部石村|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Shibi Mitaji|ja|志斐三田次|12=draft}}, who were among the educators of the crown prince, later Emperor Shōmu.
Initially, the classification of academic departments within the Daigakuryō was not well-defined.<ref>The designation of specific academic departments within the Daigakuryō, including the use of the term "Sandō," is believed to have begun during the Jōgan period in the Heian era. Before this, the term "Sanka" (算科) was used. However, this article standardizes the terminology to "Sandō."</ref> Unlike the lectures on Confucianism (later known as {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道|12=draft}}), which served as the main curriculum for training general officials, the disciplines of {{ill|Calligraphy|ja|書道 (大学寮)|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ondō|ja|音道|12=draft}} were supplementary. In contrast, Sandō focused on fields unrelated to Confucianism, such as calculation and surveying, to train technical officials, making it the only department independent of the core curriculum. However, this traditional view has been challenged by recent scholarship.
The Daigakuryō had two professors, known as '''Sandokushi''' (算博士, equivalent to Junior Seventh Rank Upper Grade), and 30 students, known as '''Sanshō''' (算生). This was equivalent to the arithmetic studies (算学) at China's {{ill|Guozijian|ja|国子監|12=draft}}. Compared to the principal department at the Guozijian, which had one professor, two assistants, multiple lecturers, and 400 students, Sandō was smaller. However, the Guozijian itself was significantly larger than the Daigakuryō. Moreover, the Sandokushi in Japan were higher-ranked (Junior Seventh Rank Upper Grade) compared to their Chinese counterparts, who held the lower rank of Junior Ninth Rank Lower Grade.
From the 2nd year of Tenpyō (730), two '''Sandotoku gyōsei''' (算得業生), or special scholarship students, were selected from among the Sanshō as candidates for Sandokushi. Additionally, from the 1st year of Tenpyō-hōji (757), students known as '''Rekisanshō''' (暦算生), who studied arithmetic necessary for calendar studies in the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|12=draft}}, were enrolled in Sandō. However, recent scholarship has criticized this understanding, noting the lack of evidence of active Sandokushi in the Nara period, with most references to arithmetic expertise pointing to graduates of {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=draft}}.<ref>For instance, in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|12=draft}}, Yamaguchi Imiki Tanushi, Shibe Ren Mitaji, and Shibe no Kashira Ishimura, recognized as "masters of arithmetic" in Yōrō 5 (721), were later instructed to teach disciples about planetary movements and calendar distribution in Tenpyō 2 (730). Additionally, Yamaguchi is documented as an "Onmyō Calendar Professor" in two documents from the Wado era.</ref> Some scholars argue that the establishment of Rekisanshō in Tenpyō-hōji 1 was part of a political integration of Sandō into the {{ill|Taishi Bureau|ja|太史局|12=draft}}.<ref>Hosoi Hiroshi, "The Arithmetic Education System in the Nara Period," *Nihon Rekishi*, Vol. 677, October 2004.</ref>
Within the Daigakuryō, a facility known as the '''Sandōin''' (算道院) was established for Sandō lectures and Sanshō accommodation. After the relocation to {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京|12=draft}}, Sandōin was located south of the Meikyōdō facilities and north of the facilities for {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道|12=draft}}.<ref>Myōhōdō (initially called Myōhōka) was established alongside the Kidenka (later Kiden-dō) during the Jinki era (724–729).</ref> Available records suggest that many Sanshō came from clans of {{ill|Toraijin|ja|渡来人|12=draft}} descent.
=== Curriculum === The textbooks for Sandō as defined in the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令|12=draft}} included the following nine works: * '''{{ill|Sunzi Suanjing|ja|孫子算経|12=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Wucao Suanjing|ja|五曹算経|12=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiuzhang Suanshu|ja|九章算術|12=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Haidao Suanjing|ja|海島算経|12=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Liuzhang|ja|六章|12=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhuishu|ja|綴術|12=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Sankaichōsa|ja|三開重差|12=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhoubi Suanjing|ja|周髀算経|12=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiusi|ja|九司|12=draft}}'''
While these works partially overlapped with the Chinese Ten Computational Classics (算経十書), certain texts like {{ill|Zhang Qiujian Suanjing|ja|張邱建算経|12=draft}} were excluded, likely due to transmission circumstances and content relevance.
== Decline of Sandō == Despite its prominence in the early Daigakuryō, Sandō gradually lost status due to shifts in political and educational priorities. By the early Heian period, Sandō had been overshadowed by fields such as {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道|12=draft}}. The reduction of Sanshō from 30 to 20 in Enryaku 21 (802) marked the beginning of its decline.
== Legacy == Sandō left an indelible mark on Japanese education, but its influence waned as mathematical pursuits fell out of favor until the emergence of {{ill|Wasan|ja|和算|12=draft}} during the early modern period.
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Sanshi|ja|算師|12=draft}} * {{ill|Mathematics history|ja|数学史|12=draft}} * {{ill|Wasan|ja|和算|12=draft}}

The same proposal also addre ... 4 KB (573 words) - 23:51, 17 January 2025 * [[Calendar Science]] ...sciplines such as * {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=draft}} and * {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道|12=draft}} hindered its development. ... 5 KB (684 words) - 01:01, 18 January 2025 * [[Reki Hakase|Reki Hakase]] ...families versed in * {{ill|暦道|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=draft}} (calendrical studies) and * {{ill|算道|ja|算道|12=draft}} (mathematical studies). Over time, native Japanese families such as the { ... 13 KB (1,579 words) - 00:47, 18 January 2025 {{Mathematics}}

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2015th year 6月}} '''Sandō''' (算道) was the department in the {{ill|Daigakuryō|ja|大学寮|12=draft}} under Japan's {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|12=draft}}, where the study of {{ill|arithmetic|ja|算術|12=draft}} was conducted.
== Overview == === Formation Period === Sandō was established at the time of the Daigakuryō's creation under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令|12=draft}}. Notable early scholars of Sandō included {{ill|Yamaguchi Tanushi|ja|山口田主|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shibe Ishimura|ja|私部石村|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Shibi Mitaji|ja|志斐三田次|12=draft}}, who were among the educators of the crown prince, later Emperor Shōmu.
Initially, the classification of academic departments within the Daigakuryō was not well-defined.<ref>The designation of specific academic departments within the Daigakuryō, including the use of the term "Sandō," is believed to have begun during the Jōgan period in the Heian era. Before this, the term "Sanka" (算科) was used. However, this article standardizes the terminology to "Sandō."</ref> Unlike the lectures on Confucianism (later known as {{ill|Meikyōdō|ja|明経道|12=draft}}), which served as the main curriculum for training general officials, the disciplines of {{ill|Calligraphy|ja|書道 (大学寮)|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ondō|ja|音道|12=draft}} were supplementary. In contrast, Sandō focused on fields unrelated to Confucianism, such as calculation and surveying, to train technical officials, making it the only department independent of the core curriculum. However, this traditional view has been challenged by recent scholarship.
The Daigakuryō had two professors, known as '''Sandokushi''' (算博士, equivalent to Junior Seventh Rank Upper Grade), and 30 students, known as '''Sanshō''' (算生). This was equivalent to the arithmetic studies (算学) at China's {{ill|Guozijian|ja|国子監|12=draft}}. Compared to the principal department at the Guozijian, which had one professor, two assistants, multiple lecturers, and 400 students, Sandō was smaller. However, the Guozijian itself was significantly larger than the Daigakuryō. Moreover, the Sandokushi in Japan were higher-ranked (Junior Seventh Rank Upper Grade) compared to their Chinese counterparts, who held the lower rank of Junior Ninth Rank Lower Grade.
From the 2nd year of Tenpyō (730), two '''Sandotoku gyōsei''' (算得業生), or special scholarship students, were selected from among the Sanshō as candidates for Sandokushi. Additionally, from the 1st year of Tenpyō-hōji (757), students known as '''Rekisanshō''' (暦算生), who studied arithmetic necessary for calendar studies in the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|12=draft}}, were enrolled in Sandō. However, recent scholarship has criticized this understanding, noting the lack of evidence of active Sandokushi in the Nara period, with most references to arithmetic expertise pointing to graduates of {{ill|Rekidō|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=draft}}.<ref>For instance, in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|12=draft}}, Yamaguchi Imiki Tanushi, Shibe Ren Mitaji, and Shibe no Kashira Ishimura, recognized as "masters of arithmetic" in Yōrō 5 (721), were later instructed to teach disciples about planetary movements and calendar distribution in Tenpyō 2 (730). Additionally, Yamaguchi is documented as an "Onmyō Calendar Professor" in two documents from the Wado era.</ref> Some scholars argue that the establishment of Rekisanshō in Tenpyō-hōji 1 was part of a political integration of Sandō into the {{ill|Taishi Bureau|ja|太史局|12=draft}}.<ref>Hosoi Hiroshi, "The Arithmetic Education System in the Nara Period," *Nihon Rekishi*, Vol. 677, October 2004.</ref>
Within the Daigakuryō, a facility known as the '''Sandōin''' (算道院) was established for Sandō lectures and Sanshō accommodation. After the relocation to {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京|12=draft}}, Sandōin was located south of the Meikyōdō facilities and north of the facilities for {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道|12=draft}}.<ref>Myōhōdō (initially called Myōhōka) was established alongside the Kidenka (later Kiden-dō) during the Jinki era (724–729).</ref> Available records suggest that many Sanshō came from clans of {{ill|Toraijin|ja|渡来人|12=draft}} descent.
=== Curriculum === The textbooks for Sandō as defined in the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令|12=draft}} included the following nine works: * '''{{ill|Sunzi Suanjing|ja|孫子算経|12=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Wucao Suanjing|ja|五曹算経|12=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiuzhang Suanshu|ja|九章算術|12=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Haidao Suanjing|ja|海島算経|12=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Liuzhang|ja|六章|12=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhuishu|ja|綴術|12=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Sankaichōsa|ja|三開重差|12=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Zhoubi Suanjing|ja|周髀算経|12=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Jiusi|ja|九司|12=draft}}'''
While these works partially overlapped with the Chinese Ten Computational Classics (算経十書), certain texts like {{ill|Zhang Qiujian Suanjing|ja|張邱建算経|12=draft}} were excluded, likely due to transmission circumstances and content relevance.
== Decline of Sandō == Despite its prominence in the early Daigakuryō, Sandō gradually lost status due to shifts in political and educational priorities. By the early Heian period, Sandō had been overshadowed by fields such as {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kidendō|ja|紀伝道|12=draft}}. The reduction of Sanshō from 30 to 20 in Enryaku 21 (802) marked the beginning of its decline.
== Legacy == Sandō left an indelible mark on Japanese education, but its influence waned as mathematical pursuits fell out of favor until the emergence of {{ill|Wasan|ja|和算|12=draft}} during the early modern period.
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Sanshi|ja|算師|12=draft}} * {{ill|Mathematics history|ja|数学史|12=draft}} * {{ill|Wasan|ja|和算|12=draft}} * [[Tokugōshō]] ...ents (2), * {{ill|Myōhōdō|ja|明法道|12=draft}} (law) students (2), and * {{ill|Sandomyō|ja|算道|12=draft}} (mathematics) students (2) as ''Tokugōshō''. The same proposal also addre ... 4 KB (573 words) - 23:51, 17 January 2025 * [[Calendar Science]] ...sciplines such as * {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=draft}} and * {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道|12=draft}} hindered its development. ... 5 KB (684 words) - 01:01, 18 January 2025 * [[Reki Hakase|Reki Hakase]] ...families versed in * {{ill|暦道|ja|暦道|1=Rekidō|12=draft}} (calendrical studies) and * {{ill|算道|ja|算道|12=draft}} (mathematical studies). Over time, native Japanese families such as the { ... 13 KB (1,579 words) - 00:47, 18 January 2025 {{Mathematics}}
